name: InfluxDB API
what_it_does: Purpose-built time-series database with HTTP API supporting Flux and SQL query languages, optimized for high-write-throughput metrics, monitoring, and IoT sensor data.
use_cases: Storing and querying infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, disk I/O) from monitoring pipelines
IoT sensor data ingestion at high frequency with time-window aggregations
Real-time dashboarding and alerting for operational metrics
Agent telemetry collection — tracking agent run durations, tool call counts, error rates
Storing downsampled time-series data with automated retention policies
not_for: Relational or document data without a strong time dimension
Workloads requiring SQL JOINs across entities (Timescale or Postgres is better)
Applications that need ACID transactions across multiple measurements
Teams deeply invested in PostgreSQL tooling who can use TimescaleDB instead
best_when: You need to ingest high-frequency time-stamped metrics with automatic downsampling and retention policies, and want a purpose-built store rather than a general-purpose database.
avoid_when: Your data has complex relational structure, or your team prefers PostgreSQL-compatible tooling and SQL — TimescaleDB will feel more natural.

auth.notes: Authentication via API token passed as Authorization: Token <token> header. Tokens can be scoped to specific buckets with read/write permissions. All-access tokens also available. InfluxDB v1 used username/password but v2/v3 use token-based auth exclusively. Tokens are non-expiring by default.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Self-host InfluxDB OSS for free. InfluxDB Cloud free tier: 30-day data retention, 5MB/5min write limit, 300MB/5min query limit
pricing.paid_tiers: InfluxDB Cloud: Usage-based from ~$0.002/MB written; serverless plans available. Clustered (enterprise) requires custom pricing
pricing.notes: OSS is fully free. InfluxDB Cloud free tier is limited primarily by 30-day retention — data older than 30 days is deleted. Paid tiers extend retention to unlimited and raise throughput limits. InfluxDB 3.x (IOx) is a newer engine with SQL support.

performance.rate_limits: Free: 5MB/5min writes; paid: varies by plan

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: HTTP API returns JSON error bodies with message and code fields. Write errors (bad line protocol format) return clear field-level errors. Rate limit errors return 429 with Retry-After. Flux query errors can be cryptic for non-Flux users — error messages reflect the Flux execution model.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Line protocol writes with the same timestamp and tags overwrite existing data points — effectively idempotent for point updates. Without explicit timestamps, writes use server time and are not idempotent. Delete operations are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Flux and SQL (InfluxDB 3.x) are two very different query languages — documentation mixes versions and languages, causing confusion
Line protocol is the write format — JSON writes are not natively supported; agents must format data correctly
Bucket names and org names are required for most API calls — must be fetched or configured separately from the token
Free cloud tier 30-day retention silently drops old data — agents must not rely on historical data past retention window
InfluxDB v1, v2, and v3 APIs are significantly different — copy-pasted code often targets wrong version
Timestamps default to nanosecond precision — sending milliseconds without specifying precision causes data to appear at wrong times
Downsampling tasks (Flux tasks) must be manually created — there's no automatic rollup without defining it

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on InfluxDB Cloud; optional on self-hosted (must be configured). Token scoping per bucket is good for least-privilege access. Tokens do not expire by default — rotation must be managed manually. Self-hosted installs with default config often lack TLS.
